claim: We introduce a biologically constrained cellular-sheaf framework for glioma multi-omics inconsistency.
support: constructed SRIS, learned maps, Laplacians, and edge residuals across Phases 1-2

claim: Subtype and grade groups exhibit statistically non-random sheaf Laplacian geometry under internal permutation tests.
support: Phase 4 permutation tests, p=0.0099 across strict protocols

claim: Sheaf residual signatures exhibit non-random OT-calibrated transport gaps between biological groups.
Consensus sheaf features add measurable strict grade-classification signal in internal cross-validation.
support: Phase 6 strict grade-label macro-F1 +0.0377 and balanced accuracy +0.0308

claim: The framework improves survival prediction over clinical-molecular baselines.
support: Phase 3 C-index gain is only about +0.0011; describe as survival-associated, not clinically superior

claim: The method is state of the art for glioma survival prediction.
support: requires external TCGA-to-CGGA validation and stronger C-index/AUROC gains

claim: This is the first use of sheaves in machine learning.
support: sheaf neural networks/neural sheaf diffusion already exist; our novelty is glioma-specific residual geometry
